canonical_definition: Across the surveyed literature, C4 (Colossal Clean Crawled Corpus) is consistently presented as a large-scale dataset derived from web crawls, with its most prevalent application being the evaluation of language model capabilities. The dominant use of C4 is to measure `language modeling` and `text generation`, which is almost universally operationalized through the perplexity metric, as seen in numerous papers on model quantization and pruning. A second, frequently mentioned role for C4 is as a source of calibration data for model compression techniques; for instance, one paper states, "We use C4 (Raffel et al., 2020) as the calibration dataset" ("SpQR: A Sparse-Quantized Representation for Near-Lossless LLM Weight Compression"). A smaller but notable body of work also utilizes C4 as a corpus for `language model pre-training` and continual training. Less commonly, papers employ C4 as a source of prompts for open-ended text generation tasks. The dataset is also occasionally used to evaluate other behaviors like `length generalization` and `robustness and stability`.

relationships.paper: SpQR: A Sparse-Quantized Representation for Near-Lossless LLM Weight Compression
relationships.evidence: We evaluate quantized model performance by two metrics. Firstly, we measure perplexity, measured on the WikiText2 (Merity et al., 2016), Penn Treebank (Marcus et al., 1994) and C4 (Raffel et al., 2020) datasets. (Section 5)

characterization: Across the surveyed literature, C4 (Colossal Clean Crawled Corpus) is consistently presented as a large-scale dataset derived from web crawls, with its most prevalent application being the evaluation of language model capabilities. The dominant use of C4 is to measure `language modeling` and `text generation`, which is almost universally operationalized through the perplexity metric, as seen in numerous papers on model quantization and pruning. A second, frequently mentioned role for C4 is as a source of calibration data for model compression techniques; for instance, one paper states, "We use C4 (Raffel et al., 2020) as the calibration dataset" ("SpQR: A Sparse-Quantized Representation for Near-Lossless LLM Weight Compression"). A smaller but notable body of work also utilizes C4 as a corpus for `language model pre-training` and continual training. Less commonly, papers employ C4 as a source of prompts for open-ended text generation tasks. The dataset is also occasionally used to evaluate other behaviors like `length generalization` and `robustness and stability`.
